content.problemStatement:
On a sunny day, Akbar and Birbal were taking a leisurely walk in palace gardens. Suddenly, Akbar noticed a bunch of sticks on the ground and decided to test Birbal's wits.

There are NNN stick holders with negligible size (numbered 111 through NNN) in a row on the ground. Akbar places all the sticks in them vertically; for each valid iii, the initial height of the stick in the iii-th holder is AiA_iAi​. Birbal has a stick cutter and his task is to completely cut all these sticks, i.e. reduce the heights of all sticks to 000. He may perform zero or more operations; in each operation, he should do the following:


Choose an integer HHH and fix the cutter at the height HHH above the ground.
The cutter moves from the 111-st to the NNN-th stick holder. Whenever it encounters a stick whose current height is greater than HHH, it cuts this stick down to height HHH (i.e. for a stick with height h>Hh \gt Hh>H, it removes its upper part with length h−Hh-Hh−H).
All the upper parts of sticks that are cut in one operation must have equal lengths. Otherwise, the operation may not be performed.
For example, if the heights of sticks are initially [5,3,5][5, 3, 5][5,3,5], then some valid values for HHH in the first operation are 333 and 444 ― the cutter cuts the upper parts of two sticks and their lengths are [2,2][2, 2][2,2] and [1,1][1, 1][1,1] respectively. H=2H = 2H=2 is an invalid choice because it would cut the upper parts of all three sticks with lengths [3,1,3][3, 1, 3][3,1,3], which are not all equal.

Akbar wants Birbal to completely cut all sticks in the minimum possible number of operations. If you want to be friends with Birbal, help him solve the problem.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of sticks. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN, where Ai is the initial height of the i‑th stick.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the minimum number of operations required to cut all sticks to height 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The distinct positive heights are {1,2,3}. Each requires one operation, so the minimum number of operations is 3.
All sticks are already at height 0, so no operation is needed.
Only one distinct positive height (5) exists, so a single operation cutting all sticks directly to 0 suffices.

solution.approach:
In one operation the cutter is set at height H. It will cut **every** stick whose current height is larger than H, and all those sticks must have the same current height because the removed upper parts must be equal. Therefore an operation can only lower the sticks that currently have the maximum height, and they must all be lowered to the next lower height that exists among the sticks (or to 0). Consequently, each distinct positive height value requires exactly one operation: we first cut all sticks of the highest height down to the second highest, then those down to the third highest, and so on, finally cutting the smallest positive height to 0. Hence the answer is simply the number of distinct heights greater than zero.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N and the array A.
2. Insert every Ai > 0 into a hash set (or sort and count distinct values).
3. The size of the set is the answer.

Complexities: O(N) time and O(N) additional memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of sticks.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) in the worst case for storing distinct heights.
